text: The surgeon uses a robotic system to perform a lower anterior resection for distal sigmoid cancer. The large uterus is suspended to facilitate access. A sponge is placed to retract the small bowel away from the medial aspect of the sigmoid mesentery, and the medial to lateral dissection begins.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) for high ligation, using scissors to clear the vascular adventitia and expose the vessel wall. The vessel sealer is then used for the ligation. The upper rectum is mobilized, with the hypogastric plexus nerves visible near the bedside. The surgeon performs lateral dissection to join the previously dissected medial plane.

The surgeon uses a sponge to identify the previously dissected area, ensuring it is positioned above the ureter. Scissors are employed for the majority of the lateral dissection, transitioning to a vessel sealer near the splenic flexure for its grasping ability and hemostatic properties. The dissection proceeds over the left kidney towards the flexure, addressing congenital adhesions of the omentum to the left anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon identifies and dissects a splenial at the top of the left kidney, distinct from the spleen itself.

The surgeon mobilizes the left side of the rectum, having previously completed the dissection on the right. A 12mm trocar is placed through the right rectus for improved stapling angle in the pelvis. The proximal mesentery is divided, showing visible blood flow, followed by the division of the distal mesentery using a stapler.

The surgeon uses a 60mm stapler to elevate the uterus and places an EEA sizer into the posterior vagina, ensuring it is positioned away from the anterior rectal wall. The posterior fornix of the vagina is opened high in the pouch of Douglas. A 15mm Endocatch bag is introduced to extract the specimen, with the bag opened outside the patient and the staple line grasped with a ring clamp for smooth removal.

The surgeon addresses bleeding at the corner of the vagina by closing the muscular wall using a 3-0 V-Loc suture. The vaginotomy is larger than the initial incision due to tearing from the specimen extraction. The camera is positioned in the epigastric midline, and suturing is performed with arms number one and three. The peritoneum is closed after the muscular wall, with the left ureter visible at the ten to eleven o'clock position.

The surgeon uses a rigid scope to inspect the rectum for any stitching or tearing after transvaginal extraction, confirming none occurred. The sewn anastomosis is aligned, with the posterior rows of staple ends joined using a 2-0 PDS suture. The inner layer is sewn with a 3-0 V-Lock suture, extending to the left and right.

The surgeon completes the second outer layer of suturing over the inner layer using IIOPDS, taking precise bites of the bowel wall without tension, ensuring the bowel comes together smoothly. The ureter is visible as the camera is repositioned to arm number three for the anastomosis. A leak test is performed using a nineteen millimeter proctoscope, confirming the anastomosis is hemostatic and widely patent with no leaks. The proximity of the rectal anastomosis and vaginal suture line is noted, and the surgeon plans to suture the omentum to the pouch of Douglas for additional support.

The surgeon uses arm number one to hold the omentum in place, interposing it between the rectal and vaginal suture lines. A second stitch is placed for reinforcement. The uterus is then dropped down, and bipolar cautery is applied to ensure hemostasis at the suture line.
